body: This document exists to define how UX JSON items should reference each other without creating hidden gaps, duplicate authority, or broken hierarchy traces. Use it when authoring relationships, current-to-desired mappings, coverage indexes, pending decisions, or acceptance criteria.
References are part of the UX source of truth. Broken or ambiguous refs should be treated as corpus drift.

content.title: ID Rules
body: IDs should be stable, readable, and unique within the repo or corpus namespace.
Prefer dot-separated IDs that include the domain and behavior concept, such as:
```text
jobs.filters.desired.modal-apply
Do not make IDs depend on file location, generated Markdown path, implementation file path, or current component names.

content.title: Parent And Child Refs
body: Hierarchy refs should support this trace where the corpus depth requires it:
```text
feature -> subfeature -> journey -> flow -> subflow -> requirement -> observable scenario -> acceptance criterion

Child items should point to their parent when the parent exists. Parent items may list children for navigation and review, but child-to-parent refs are the more important integrity path because split/compose workflows may move child files independently.
If a child belongs under multiple parents, prefer creating a relationship ref and explain the primary governing parent. Do not duplicate the child as two governing items.

content.title: Relationship Refs
body: Relationship refs should describe UX meaning, not implementation dependency.
Use relationship refs for:
- parent/child hierarchy
- related requirements
- prerequisite UX behavior
- dependent UX behavior
- acceptance criteria
- pending decisions
- current/desired mapping links
- row-inline behaviorRelations field links
- runtime drift links
Do not use relationship refs to encode controller imports, store ownership, hook composition, backend endpoints, or implementation sequencing.

content.title: Current-To-Desired Mapping Refs
body: Mappings must support many-to-many relationships.
Use arrays for both `currentRefs` and `desiredRefs`, even when the mapping is one-to-one, so split, merge, replacement, added, and removed behavior can use the same shape.
Mapping refs should identify the relevant mode. A desired behavior change should not be inferred from a current row unless a mapping records the decision.

content.title: Coverage Refs
body: Coverage rows should reference the item they cover and, when relevant, the parent item that makes the coverage meaningful.
Coverage should expose unresolved refs instead of hiding them. Use:
- `missingChildren` for expected child branches that do not exist yet
- `pendingDecisionRefs` for unresolved behavior or confirmation
- `acceptanceCriterionRefs` for proof rows
- coverage statuses such as `missing`, `pending`, `needs-confirmation`, or `needs-proof`
Do not mark a branch as `covered` when important child refs are missing.

content.title: Pending Decision Refs
body: Pending decisions should reference affected requirements, mappings, flows, subflows, observable scenarios, or acceptance criteria where practical.
If a pending decision blocks implementation, the affected artifact should also expose that block through implementation gate or coverage status. Do not rely on the pending decision item alone.

content.title: Acceptance Criterion Refs
body: Acceptance criteria should point to the behavior they prove.
Prefer linking acceptance criteria to the lowest meaningful governing object:
- requirement when proving a behavior concept
- observable scenario when proving a user-visible state
- subflow when proving a coherent user action sequence
- flow when the proof spans multiple subflows
Do not create acceptance criteria that prove implementation details instead of UX behavior.

content.title: Broken Reference Handling
body: When an agent finds a broken, ambiguous, or duplicated reference, it should classify the issue as one of:
- doc drift
- missing artifact
- pending ownership decision
- pending behavior decision
- intentionally omitted branch
- stale generated output
The agent should fix the ref when the correct target is clear. If the correct target is not clear, it should route the issue through a pending decision or coverage row rather than guessing.
